title: The Core of Calvinism, Part 3b: Corporate Worship
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 7–28-24 - The Core of Calvinism 3b - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Introduction: Worship Must Be at the Center
subpoints: The Reformed believer loves the corporate worship of the Lord
An age of individualism must be confronted: it is 'us and Jesus,' not just 'me and Jesus'
Watching a video on a device is not the same as gathered corporate worship
The Calvinist holds corporate worship in high priority because there is no higher calling than worshiping God

outline.title: Everyone Has an Urge to Worship
subpoints: Romans 1:20: creation clearly reveals God, leaving all without excuse
Because of sin, humanity suppresses this truth and redirects the urge to worship toward creation (Romans 1:21-23)
The Pacific Northwest's religion is environmentalism — the worship of nature
God not only instituted that he must be worshiped, but also prescribed how he must be worshiped

outline.title: The Regulative Principle of Worship
subpoints: Deuteronomy 12:29-31: Do not worship the Lord your God in the way the nations worship their gods
The regulative principle: we only do in corporate worship what God commands us to do
The alternative (anything not forbidden is permitted) leads to pleasing people rather than God
The seeker-sensitive movement literally surveyed unbelievers to shape the Lord's Day service

outline.title: God Alone Is the Object of Worship
subpoints: Matthew 4:10: "You shall worship the Lord your God and Him only you shall serve"
The Roman Catholic Church officially teaches three levels of worship — latria for God, hyperdulia for Mary, dulia for saints — which the Bible forbids
God can only be worshiped through Christ the mediator (John 14:6; 1 Timothy 2:5)
No Muslim, Jew, or adherent of any religion without Christ can worship the true God

outline.title: The Ordinary Elements of Worship
subpoints: Prayer: directed to the Father, through the Son, with the help of the Spirit; in a known language (1 Corinthians 14:12-15)
Reading of Scripture: a separate, stand-alone element — not merely a prelude to preaching
Preaching: the central element of Protestant worship; the primary means God uses for the Spirit's work
Singing: all Christians must sing — the Bible has no category of a non-singing Christian
Sacraments: Baptism (Matthew 28) and the Lord's Supper (1 Corinthians 11) — not salvific, but for the people of God
Church discipline: Matthew 18; announced in the worship service as an act of worship
Offering: an act of worship — the first fruits offered to God, not an intermission

outline.title: Occasional Elements of Worship
subpoints: Oaths and vows (e.g., ordinations)
Fasting
Thanksgiving upon special occasions

outline.title: Personal and Family Worship
subpoints: God requires personal, family, and public worship
Time is rarely a true excuse for not doing personal worship — it is a matter of priority
The Calvinist knows that to live for God's glory with great joy, corporate worship must be held in high priority

summary: Continuing the "Core of Calvinism" series, Part 3b takes up the topic of corporate worship. The first part establishes that the Reformed believer — one who truly believes in the sovereign God and sovereign grace — will love and prioritize the corporate worship of God, because there is no higher calling. The sermon challenges the individualism of modern Christianity by insisting that biblical worship is communal: "us and Jesus," not merely "me and Jesus." Drawing on Romans 1:20-23 and Deuteronomy 12:29-31, Pastor Tito Lyro explains the regulative principle of worship: God not only commands that he must be worshiped, but he prescribes how he must be worshiped, and to look elsewhere — to culture, to the nations, to unbeliever surveys — is to borrow from idolatry. The sermon then works through the ordinary elements of worship: prayer, Scripture reading, preaching (the central pillar of Protestant worship), singing, sacraments, church discipline, and the offering — each grounded in specific biblical texts. Occasional elements (oaths, fasting, special thanksgiving) and personal/family worship are also briefly addressed. The sermon closes with a challenge to hold corporate worship in the highest priority as the visible expression of a life lived for God's glory.
transcript:
## Introduction: Worship Must Be at the Center

All right, we're going to continue our series on the joy that a Calvinist, that a Reformed believer, should have in living life. We started a few weeks ago — not so much defining the doctrine of the Reformed faith, but talking about how that doctrine should affect the way we live. We've seen that living for the glory of God with great joy is what Calvinism does. That's what the Reformed believer does.

We saw to begin with that the marrow of Calvinism, the center of Calvinism, is the sovereignty of God, and that he works in our lives all by his grace. The result of believing in Christ is lives that are marked by piety, by holiness. And those who live a pious life love the corporate worship of the Lord. And that's our lesson for today — the Calvinist, the Reformed believer, the one who believes what the Bible says concerning salvation and God, loves the corporate worship of his God.

In an age of individualism, when everything is just "me and Jesus," we need to see that the Bible teaches it is "us and Jesus" — not just me and Jesus, but when we gather to worship, us and Jesus. And in a day where people think that watching a video on a device is worshiping God, we need to understand that our God reveals himself to us in the physical corporate worship of the church. The sacraments of the church are really hard to get wet through a screen, or to eat bread and drink wine through a screen. These are the physicality of the corporate worship of God.

So yes, the sons and daughters of the Reformation must constantly remind ourselves of the rich emphasis on corporate worship that our tradition carries — a tradition thoroughly rooted in the Word of God. The Calvinist knows that if he wants to live for God's glory with great joy, he must hold the corporate worship of God in high priority. Because there is no higher calling than worshiping God.

## Everyone Has an Urge to Worship

I want to start by saying that everybody that exists finds the urge to worship. There's not one single person who has ever lived, who lives today, or who will live before the coming of the Lord, who does not find the urge to worship. And that happens because of the way that we are created. All of creation reveals that there is a God. And that cannot be denied.

Romans 1:20: "For since the creation of the world, his invisible attributes are clearly seen — not kind of seen in a hidden way, but clearly seen — being understood by the things that are made, even his eternal power and Godhead, so that they are without excuse." Psalm 19 verses 1-6 talks about the glory of God displayed in the heavens.

Because of sin, though — Romans 1:21-23 — although they knew God, they did not glorify him as God, nor were thankful, but became futile in their thoughts, and their foolish hearts were darkened. They changed the glory of the incorruptible God into an image made like corruptible man — and birds, and four-footed animals, and creeping things. The urge to worship is so great that it manifests itself in some sort of worship, but never the worship of the true God — it comes out as worship of creation.

In our area, the supposedly most irreligious region of the United States — what is the religion of this region? The climate. Environmentalism is the worship of this region. The DSM (the diagnostic manual for mental disorders) is in the process of adding a new disorder: climate anxiety disorder. And the highest prevalence of that disorder in the world is in the Pacific Northwest.

But the revelation of God in Scripture actually tells us whom we should worship and prescribes how we are to worship him. God instituted not only that he must be worshiped, but also how he must be worshiped.

## The Regulative Principle of Worship

In Deuteronomy 12:29-31, God is speaking to the nation of Israel. He says: "When the Lord your God cuts off from before you the nations...take heed to yourself that you are not ensnared to follow them...and that you do not inquire after their gods, saying, 'How did these nations serve their gods? I will do likewise.'" And then notice the change in verse 31: "You shall not worship the Lord your God in that way." Not only are you not going to worship false gods, but you're not going to borrow from society to worship the true God either.

This is what we call the regulative principle of worship: we only do in the corporate worship of God what he commands us to do. The alternative position — that anything the Bible does not forbid is okay in worship — leads to looking for ways to worship God outside of the Scriptures, which gives yourself over to the imagination and devices of men and the suggestions of Satan. The best-intentioned person, when not controlled by the Scriptures, is very likely to follow the temptation to just please people in the worship of God.

A lot of churches have lost their focus on worshiping God this way — what people like or what people want becomes the focus of worship. In the 1990s there was the seeker-sensitive movement, where churches would literally send questionnaires to unbelievers in their area to ask them how they should shape the worship service on the Lord's Day so that they could attract them into the church: "What would make you comfortable in the church?" That is asking the peoples of the land how God should be worshiped.

## God Alone Is the Object of Worship

Just to state the obvious: God, and God alone, is the proper object of worship. Our Lord himself said when resisting Satan's temptation: "Away with you, Satan! For it is written, 'You shall worship the Lord your God, and him only you shall serve.'" The Apostle Paul says in Colossians 2 that worshiping anything other than God alone is being robbed, being cheated of your reward.

Now different people have different ideas of who God is and how he should be worshiped. The Roman Catholic Church officially teaches three levels of worship: latria (highest worship) only for the Godhead, hyperdulia for Mary, and dulia for the saints. But when the Bible says "Don't worship anybody else but me" — "You shall have no other gods before me...you shall not bow down to them nor serve them" — worship of anything at any level other than God is an act of hatred toward God.

God can only be worshiped through Christ the mediator. The Lord Jesus Christ himself says, "I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me." Paul says, "There is one God and one mediator between God and man, the man Christ Jesus." The Muslim cannot worship the true God of the Bible. The Jew cannot worship the true God of the Bible — or any other religion that does not acknowledge Christ as the mediator.

## The Ordinary Elements of Worship

How does God want to be worshiped? What is it that he wants in a worship service?

He wants his people to pray. All prayers, according to Scripture, must be directed to the Father through the mediation of the Son, with the help of the Spirit. And if prayer is corporate, it must be in a known language — Paul says that clearly in 1 Corinthians 14:12-15.

The next ordinary act of worship is the reading of the Bible. If you notice in our service, we don't include the reading of the Bible in the sermon — it's a separate item. Reading the Bible in public is an act of worship in and of itself. A seminary professor once said, for the sake of time, he was going to skip the reading of the Scriptures and go straight to the sermon. The reply was: you're not graduating, because you just skipped the only inspired thing you're going to say all morning.

Then the preaching of the Word — the central part of Protestant corporate worship. Paul makes that point in 1 Corinthians 1:17-18: the preaching of the word is foolishness to those who are perishing, but for those in Christ, it is the power of God. In old Protestant churches, the pulpit was central to the architecture — preaching was at the center. We've lost that now, more concerned about other things.

The Larger Catechism, Question 155: "How is the word made effectual to salvation? The Spirit of God makes the reading, but especially the preaching of the word, an effectual means of enlightening, convincing, and humbling sinners...of building them up in grace and establishing their hearts in holiness and comfort through faith unto salvation." As profitable as it is to read your Bible every day, as profitable as it is to listen to podcasts of preaching, it is the preaching on the Lord's Day that is the primary tool God uses to accomplish these things.

Another ordinary act of worship is singing. Brothers and sisters, if you don't sing and claim to be a Christian, as simple as that — if you're not singing in the worship service, there is no biblical category of a Christian who doesn't sing. "I don't have a good voice." Who cares? God gave you whatever talents you have to use in worshiping him.

Our confession seems to imply that only Psalms should be used, but the Bible does not command the church to sing only Psalms. The Old Testament church didn't sing only Psalms. The New Testament evidence of apostolic singing is that they sang a hymn (Acts 16 — though not in a church service). In Revelation we have singing in heaven in which the saints sing a new song — which is not a Psalm.

Another part of ordinary worship is the administration of the sacraments. We have two: Baptism, instituted in Matthew 28 in the Great Commission, and the Lord's Supper, from the Last Supper and repeated in 1 Corinthians 11. The sacraments are not salvific ordinances — they're not given for the salvation of people. They're given for those who belong to God's people.

Church discipline is also an act of ordinary worship. Remember Matthew 18 — when the brother doesn't repent and you've gone through the process, you tell it to the church. That's an act of worship. Our Larger Catechism explicitly lists "church government and discipline" among the elements of worship.

And then one last item of ordinary worship is the offering. The Larger Catechism says that the "maintenance" of ministry is an element of worship. That's why we take an offering during our worship service — it's a time of worship. We're offering to God the first fruits of what he has given us. It's not an intermission. It's not a potty break. It is an act of time of worship.

## Occasional Elements of Worship

The Scripture also speaks of occasional acts of worship — elements we don't do all the time: taking oaths and vows (ordinations are done as part of the worship service), fasting (a private thing that is a personal act of worship), and Thanksgiving upon special occasions.

## Personal and Family Worship

God requires personal worship, family worship, and public worship. Public worship on the Lord's Day is essential. But also personal and family worship — reading the Scriptures, praying by yourself, praying with your family.

Sometimes we say that we don't have time for personal worship. But when we say that, we are actually saying that we don't think of it as a priority. We have time to watch TV, to read books, to go to the movies, to be involved in sports. Time is rarely a real excuse for not doing personal worship — it is a matter of priority.

So the worship of God must be taken very seriously, done in accordance with the Bible, because the Calvinist knows that if he wants to live for God's glory with great joy, he must hold the corporate worship of God in high priority.

Father in heaven, thank you so much for your desire for us to worship you. And we thank you that even when we err, you forgive us because of Jesus Christ. We pray you would go before us as we now break from Sunday school and get ready to worship you this morning. In Jesus' name, amen.
